title: Yayoi Kusama's Infinity Rooms Continue to Prove That Great Art Needs No Explanation
author: Monica Cosby
body:
[youtube:0-C69t22twQ]
## The Tour That Will Not End

Yayoi Kusama's infinity mirror rooms have been touring global institutions for years now, and the demand shows no sign of diminishing. Through 2023 and into 2024, exhibitions featuring her immersive installations continued to sell out across multiple continents, with wait times for timed entry slots stretching into months. In an art world that constantly chases the next thing, Kusama's enduring popularity is both remarkable and instructive.

The numbers tell a partial story. Tate Modern extended its infinity mirror rooms exhibition through April 2024 after overwhelming demand. SFMOMA ran Yayoi Kusama: Infinite Love with two installations including the brand-new Dreaming of Earth's Sphericity, I Would Offer My Love, completed in 2023. The Pérez Art Museum Miami presented LOVE IS CALLING, one of the largest and most physically expansive of all the infinity rooms, for over a year. Each new venue sells out before critics have finished reviewing it.

Kusama, born in Matsumoto, Japan in 1929, began making her infinity rooms in 1965 with Infinity Mirror Room, Phalli's Field. Over the following six decades she has produced more than twenty distinct iterations. She has been working in this mode longer than most of her current visitors have been alive, and the rooms keep generating the same response: awe that bypasses comprehension and goes straight to the body.

## The Experience Itself

For those who have not yet stepped inside one, an infinity mirror room is difficult to describe adequately. The mirrored walls create the illusion of infinite space, and the carefully positioned lights or objects within that space are reflected endlessly in every direction. The visitor stands inside what appears to be a boundless cosmos. The effect is immediate, visceral, and profoundly moving in a way that has nothing to do with intellectual understanding.

This is the key to Kusama's mass appeal. Her installations do not require art historical knowledge or critical vocabulary to appreciate. They operate on a sensory level that is accessible to anyone who walks through the door. A five-year-old and an art history professor will have different frameworks for processing the experience, but both will feel something genuine.

Kusama has spoken for decades about the visual and auditory hallucinations she experienced from childhood, about the way repetition and multiplication of form was a way of managing and externalizing that experience. The polka dots and the reflections are not decorative. They are the external form of an internal state, transformed into something shareable. The viewer inside the room is not just experiencing art. They are, briefly, experiencing the world as Kusama experiences it. That is a rare and profound transfer.

## The Social Media Question

Critics have raised the question of whether the popularity of infinity rooms is driven primarily by their Instagram-friendliness. The rooms are undeniably photogenic, and social media has certainly amplified their visibility. But dismissing the work as mere content fodder is condescending to both the artist and her audience.

People photograph things that move them. The impulse to capture and share the experience of standing inside infinite reflected light is not a corruption of the artistic encounter. It is a natural extension of it. Kusama herself has spoken about wanting to dissolve the boundaries between self and universe, and the act of sharing that dissolution with others through photography is entirely consistent with her artistic philosophy.

The more honest version of the social media critique is about what happens before the room. The two-minute timed entry slot, the management of crowds, the way institutional infrastructure now shapes the encounter before it begins: these are real questions about how the experience is being delivered. They are not questions about whether the experience, once had, is genuine. It is. Kusama's work survives the institutional packaging because the rooms work on the nervous system faster than any management system can neutralize.

## An Artist Beyond Categorization

Kusama, now in her nineties, continues to produce work at a pace that would exhaust artists half her age. Her studio output remains prolific, and each new exhibition demonstrates that her creative vision has not diminished. The infinity rooms are the most visible expression of a practice that spans painting, sculpture, film, and writing over seven decades.

Her continued relevance is not a fluke. It is evidence that art which connects with fundamental human experiences transcends trends, market cycles, and critical fashion. The experience of standing inside infinite reflected light, of feeling the boundaries of the self momentarily dissolve, of being small and immense simultaneously, is not an experience that dates. It is one of the oldest human experiences in a new physical form.

In 2016, Kusama received the Order of Culture from the Japanese Imperial Family, becoming the first woman to receive that honor for painting. The recognition matters less than the work, but it confirms what the queues at every institution already demonstrate: this is not a minor figure having a cultural moment. It is a major artist whose moment has been building for sixty years and shows no sign of ending.

## The Practice Behind the Rooms

It would be easy to treat the infinity rooms in isolation, as if they existed independently of the broader practice. They don't. Kusama's studio in Tokyo continues to produce large-scale paintings, those dense fields of nets and dots and botanical forms that have their own obsessive intensity. The room-scale installations and the paintings are doing the same thing at different scales and through different means: externalizing the interior through repetition until the interior becomes the world, until self-obliteration becomes self-expression.

Self-obliteration is Kusama's term. She developed it as an artistic philosophy in the 1960s and it has shaped everything since. The idea that the ego, the bounded individual self, can be dissolved through the experience of infinite multiplication, that you can lose yourself in a field of polka dots or a room of lights until the distinction between self and world temporarily ceases to matter: this is both a psychological statement and an artistic one. The infinity rooms deliver on that promise more completely than almost anything else in contemporary art.

The philosophical depth behind the experience does not diminish the experience's accessibility. That is Kusama's achievement: work that functions on the level of pure sensation for everyone who encounters it, and rewards deeper investigation for those who want it. Both encounters are valid. Both are complete.
